title: Layer blending
content_html:
Layer blending

Layering photos in processing: is it possible to emulate the way Photoshop can blend layers together?

Yes — Processing can absolutely emulate Photoshop-style layer blending, though with some limitations compared to Photoshop’s long list of modes.

At its core, Photoshop blending works by taking pixel values from a base layer and a blend layer, then combining them according to a formula (multiply, screen, overlay, etc.). Processing gives you a few tools to do the same:

⸻

1. Using blend() function

Processing has a built-in blend() function that lets you combine two images with specific blending modes:

blend(srcImg, 0, 0, srcImg.width, srcImg.height,
      0, 0, destImg.width, destImg.height,
      MULTIPLY);  

*// options: BLEND, ADD, SUBTRACT, LIGHTEST, DARKEST, DIFFERENCE, MULTIPLY, SCREEN, OVERLAY, etc.*

Supported modes are more limited than Photoshop, but you do get ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, SCREEN, OVERLAY, DARKEST, LIGHTEST, DIFFERENCE.

⸻

2. Using tint() + image()

You can draw multiple images on top of each other with transparency:

tint(255, 127); 

*// half transparent*


image(img1, 0, 0);
image(img2, 0, 0);

This gives simple opacity control, like lowering a layer’s opacity in Photoshop.

⸻

3. Manual per-pixel blending

For full Photoshop-like control, you can access pixel arrays and apply custom formulas. For example, a Multiply blend:

img1.loadPixels();
img2.loadPixels();
PImage output = createImage(img1.width, img1.height, RGB);
output.loadPixels();

for (int i = 0; i < img1.pixels.length; i++) {
  color c1 = img1.pixels[i];
  color c2 = img2.pixels[i];
  
  float r = red(c1) * red(c2) / 255;
  float g = green(c1) * green(c2) / 255;
  float b = blue(c1) * blue(c2) / 255;
  
  output.pixels[i] = color(r, g, b);
}

output.updatePixels();
image(output, 0, 0);

With this approach, you can implement all Photoshop blend modes (Overlay, Soft Light, Hard Light, Color Burn, etc.) by using their published formulas.

⸻

4. PShader for GPU blending

If you want speed, you can write a GLSL fragment shader (Processing PShader) to do blending in real time. That way you can overlay multiple layers at 60fps, which is useful for video or interactive pieces.

⸻

✅ **Summary:** • Quick & simple: blend() for basic modes. • Opacity only: tint() + image(). • Photoshop-like control: custom per-pixel blending or shaders.

⸻

Would you like me to: • give you a ready-to-use function that implements several Photoshop blend modes in Processing (Multiply, Screen, Overlay, etc.), • or focus on how to do it efficiently with shaders for layering many photos/videos?

Initially, the aim is to create static layered composites for each permutation of n source images.

Got it — we can batch-build static composites for every permutation of n source images in Processing. Below is a ready-to-run sketch that: • Loads all images from a folder • Generates every permutation of their order (1st over 2nd over 3rd, etc.) • Blends them (Multiply/Screen/Overlay/Lighten/Darken provided) • Saves each composite with a filename that records the permutation + modes

⚠️ Heads-up: permutations grow fast (n!). e.g., 6 images ⇒ 720 outputs. Use MAX_PERMS to cap when testing, or switch to combinations if you don’t need order.

⸻

How to use 1. Create a Processing sketch and add this file as LayerPermutations.pde. 2. Put your images in a folder named data/input inside the sketch. (Any mix of JPG/PNG). 3. Run. Outputs go to output/ next to your sketch.

⸻

Processing (Java) code

*// LayerPermutations.pde*




*// Build static composites for EVERY permutation of n images.*




*// Place source images in "data/input". Outputs go to "output/".*





*// ---------- USER SETTINGS ----------*


String INPUT_DIR   = "input";       

*// relative to /data*


String OUTPUT_DIR  = "output";      

*// relative to sketch folder*


int TARGET_W = -1;                  

*// -1 = use first image width*


int TARGET_H = -1;                  

*// -1 = use first image height*


float LAYER_OPACITY = 1.0;          

*// 0..1 opacity for each added layer*


boolean RESIZE_TO_FIRST = true;     

*// force all layers to size of first image*


boolean CYCLE_MODES = true;         

*// cycle through modes list per layer*


int MAX_PERMS = -1;                 

*// -1 = no cap; e.g. set 50 while testing*





*// Choose one or more modes to use (cycled or single)*


String[] MODES = new String[]{
  "MULTIPLY", "SCREEN", "OVERLAY", "LIGHTEN", "DARKEN"
};



*// ---------- RUNTIME ----------*


ArrayList<PImage> images = new ArrayList<PImage>();
ArrayList<String> names  = new ArrayList<String>();
ArrayList<int[]> perms   = new ArrayList<int[]>();

void settings() {
  size(800, 800); 

*// canvas is unused for output; just to keep Processing happy*


}

void setup() {
  

*// Load images*


  File dir = new File(dataPath(INPUT_DIR));
  if (!dir.exists() || !dir.isDirectory()) {
    println("✖ Input folder not found: " + dir.getAbsolutePath());
    println("Create 'data/input' and add images.");
    exit();
  }

  

*// Filter supported extensions*


  String[] exts = {".png", ".jpg", ".jpeg"};
  File[] files = dir.listFiles();
  if (files == null || files.length == 0) {
    println("✖ No files in input folder.");
    exit();
  }

  for (File f : files) {
    String nm = f.getName().toLowerCase();
    if (endsWithAny(nm, exts)) {
      PImage im = loadImage(INPUT_DIR + "/" + f.getName());
      if (im == null) continue;
      images.add(im);
      names.add(f.getName());
    }
  }

  if (images.size() < 2) {
    println("✖ Need at least 2 images. Found: " + images.size());
    exit();
  }

  

*// Target size*


  if (RESIZE_TO_FIRST) {
    TARGET_W = images.get(0).width;
    TARGET_H = images.get(0).height;
  } else {
    if (TARGET_W <= 0 || TARGET_H <= 0) {
      TARGET_W = images.get(0).width;
      TARGET_H = images.get(0).height;
    }
  }

  

*// Normalize sizes*


  for (int i = 0; i < images.size(); i++) {
    PImage src = images.get(i);
    if (src.width != TARGET_W || src.height != TARGET_H) {
      PImage scaled = createImage(TARGET_W, TARGET_H, ARGB);
      scaled.copy(src, 0, 0, src.width, src.height, 0, 0, TARGET_W, TARGET_H);
      images.set(i, scaled);
    }
  }

  

*// Build permutations*


  int n = images.size();
  int[] baseIdx = new int[n];
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) baseIdx[i] = i;
  permute(baseIdx, 0);

  println("Images: " + images.size());
  println("Permutations: " + perms.size());

  

*// Ensure output dir*


  File outDir = new File(sketchPath(OUTPUT_DIR));
  outDir.mkdirs();

  

*// Process permutations*


  int produced = 0;
  for (int p = 0; p < perms.size(); p++) {
    if (MAX_PERMS > 0 && produced >= MAX_PERMS) break;

    int[] order = perms.get(p);
    PImage out = images.get(order[0]).copy();

    

*// Blend each subsequent layer*


    for (int li = 1; li < order.length; li++) {
      PImage top = images.get(order[li]);
      String mode = CYCLE_MODES ? MODES[(li - 1) % MODES.length] : MODES[0];
      out = blendTwo(out, top, mode, LAYER_OPACITY);
    }

    

*// Build filename with order + modes used*


    String ordStr = joinInt(order, "-");
    String modeStr = (CYCLE_MODES ? join(MODES, "-") : MODES[0]);
    String fname = String.format("perm_%s__modes_%s.png", ordStr, modeStr);

    out.save(sketchPath(OUTPUT_DIR + "/" + fname));
    produced++;

    if (p % 25 == 0) println("Saved: " + fname);
  }

  println("✔ Done. Wrote " + produced + " composite(s) to: " + sketchPath(OUTPUT_DIR));
  exit();
}



*// ---------- BLENDING ----------*




*// Blend top into base using mode and opacity (0..1)*


PImage blendTwo(PImage base, PImage top, String mode, float opacity) {
  PImage out = createImage(base.width, base.height, ARGB);
  base.loadPixels();
  top.loadPixels();
  out.loadPixels();

  int len = base.pixels.length;
  for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
    int cb = base.pixels[i];
    int ct = top.pixels[i];

    float br = red(cb), bg = green(cb), bb = blue(cb), ba = alpha(cb) / 255.0;
    float tr = red(ct), tg = green(ct), tb = blue(ct), ta = alpha(ct) / 255.0;

    

*// Effective alpha of top after user opacity*


    float aTop = constrain(ta * opacity, 0, 1);

    

*// Apply blend mode to RGB (0..255 math)*


    float rBlend = applyMode(br, tr, mode);
    float gBlend = applyMode(bg, tg, mode);
    float bBlend = applyMode(bb, tb, mode);

    

*// Alpha composite: out = (1 - aTop)*base + aTop*blend*


    float r = (1 - aTop) * br + aTop * rBlend;
    float g = (1 - aTop) * bg + aTop * gBlend;
    float b = (1 - aTop) * bb + aTop * bBlend;
    float a = 255 * (ba + aTop - ba * aTop); 

*// standard "over"*



    out.pixels[i] = color(constrain(r, 0, 255), constrain(g, 0, 255), constrain(b, 0, 255), constrain(a, 0, 255));
  }

  out.updatePixels();
  return out;
}



*// Apply blend mode to a single channel (0..255)*


float applyMode(float b, float t, String mode) {
  float r;
  if (mode.equals("MULTIPLY")) {
    r = (b * t) / 255.0;
  } else if (mode.equals("SCREEN")) {
    r = 255 - ((255 - b) * (255 - t)) / 255.0;
  } else if (mode.equals("OVERLAY")) {
    

*// Photoshop-style overlay*


    r = (b < 128) ? (2 * b * t / 255.0) : (255 - 2 * (255 - b) * (255 - t) / 255.0);
  } else if (mode.equals("LIGHTEN")) {
    r = max(b, t);
  } else if (mode.equals("DARKEN")) {
    r = min(b, t);
  } else if (mode.equals("DIFFERENCE")) {
    r = abs(b - t);
  } else if (mode.equals("ADD")) {
    r = b + t;          

*// will be clamped later*


  } else if (mode.equals("SUBTRACT")) {
    r = b - t;          

*// will be clamped later*


  } else if (mode.equals("SOFTLIGHT")) {
    

*// Approximate soft light*


    float tN = t / 255.0;
    r = (1 - 2 * tN) * b * b / 255.0 + 2 * tN * b;
  } else {
    

*// Fallback to normal (just use top’s value)*


    r = t;
  }
  return constrain(r, 0, 255);
}



*// ---------- PERMUTATIONS ----------*


void permute(int[] arr, int l) {
  if (l == arr.length - 1) {
    perms.add(arr.clone());
    return;
  }
  for (int i = l; i < arr.length; i++) {
    swap(arr, l, i);
    permute(arr, l + 1);
    swap(arr, l, i);
  }
}

void swap(int[] a, int i, int j) {
  int tmp = a[i]; a[i] = a[j]; a[j] = tmp;
}

boolean endsWithAny(String s, String[] suffixes) {
  for (String suf : suffixes) if (s.endsWith(suf)) return true;
  return false;
}

String joinInt(int[] arr, String sep) {
  String[] ss = new String[arr.length];
  for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) ss[i] = str(arr[i]);
  return join(ss, sep);
}

⸻

Tweaks you’ll likely want • Limit output while testing: set MAX_PERMS = 50. • Fix blend mode for all layers: set CYCLE_MODES = false and pick one in MODES[0]. • Per-layer opacities/modes: we can extend the code to read a CSV (e.g., layer,mode,opacity) and apply those settings per position in the stack. • Combinations instead of permutations: If order doesn’t matter, switch to k-combinations to cut output drastically.

If you want, tell me how many images you’ll use (e.g., 3..6), whether order really matters, and your preferred modes/opacity rules—I’ll tailor the script (including a combinations variant and a CSV-driven mode/opacity map).

Provide a processing file and a html description of the approach and overview of the code functions

All set! Here are your files: • Download the Processing sketch (.pde) • Download the HTML overview

If you want a Markdown version of the overview, a CSV-driven per-layer mode/opacity map, or a combinations (not permutations) variant, say the word and I’ll add it.

Explain difference between combinations and permutations

Great question — this distinction is exactly what matters when deciding how many composites you’ll end up generating.

⸻

Permutations • Definition: All possible orderings of a set of items. • Key: Order matters. • Formula: P(n, r) = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!} (for choosing r items from n in sequence) • Example with images {A, B, C}: • For all 3 images (r = 3): • ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA • That’s 6 permutations (= 3!).

👉 In your Processing sketch, **permutations** mean you generate a composite for every possible *stacking order* of the source images. With n images, that’s n! composites.

⸻

Combinations • Definition: All possible selections of items from a set. • Key: Order does not matter. • Formula: C(n, r) = \binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{r!(n-r)!} • Example with images {A, B, C}: • Choosing 2 at a time: • {A, B}, {A, C}, {B, C} • Only 3 combinations (instead of 6 permutations).

👉 For your composites, **combinations** mean: “Which set of images do I blend together?” without caring about their order. So {A, B, C} produces just **one composite**, not six different orderings.

⸻

Quick Comparison

Input size (n) Permutations (n!) Combinations (just one set)
3 images 6 composites 1 composite
4 images 24 composites 1 composite
6 images 720 composites 1 composite

(If you allow subsets — e.g. any 2, 3, or 4 images chosen out of 6 — the number of combinations grows, but still far more slowly than permutations.)

⸻

✅ **Summary:** • Use **permutations** if you want *every ordering* (layer A over B is different from B over A). • Use **combinations** if you only care about *which images are present*, not their order.

⸻

Do you want me to adapt the Processing sketch to switch from permutations to combinations (so {A,B,C} gives only one composite rather than six)?

Blending images needs low opacity so all the images contribute. Can processing emulate a Photoshop auto-levels or auto-contrast function?

Yes — you can get very close to Photoshop’s Auto Levels / Auto Contrast in Processing.

Photoshop’s Auto Levels basically does this: 1. Look at the histogram (distribution of brightness values) of each channel (R, G, B). 2. Find the darkest pixel (min value > 0) and the brightest pixel (max value < 255). 3. Linearly rescale everything so that the min maps to 0 and the max maps to 255. • If you use one scale across all channels, that’s like Auto Contrast (preserving color balance). • If you rescale each channel independently, that’s like Auto Levels (can shift color balance).

⸻

1. Low Opacity Blending

If you want all n layers to contribute without any one overpowering: • Just lower the opacity (e.g. 1/n for each). • In Processing:


PImage composite = createImage(width, height, RGB);
composite.loadPixels();
for (int i = 0; i < composite.pixels.length; i++) {
  float r = 0, g = 0, b = 0;
  for (int j = 0; j < images.size(); j++) {
    color c = images.get(j).pixels[i];
    r += red(c);
    g += green(c);
    b += blue(c);
  }
  r /= images.size();
  g /= images.size();
  b /= images.size();
  composite.pixels[i] = color(r, g, b);
}
composite.updatePixels();

That’s like “average blend” — equivalent to stacking all images with equal low opacity.

⸻

2. Auto Contrast / Auto Levels in Processing

Here’s a function you can drop in:


PImage autoLevels(PImage img, boolean perChannel) {
  img.loadPixels();
  PImage out = createImage(img.width, img.height, RGB);
  out.loadPixels();

  

*// find min and max*


  float minR=255, minG=255, minB=255;
  float maxR=0, maxG=0, maxB=0;
  for (int i = 0; i < img.pixels.length; i++) {
    color c = img.pixels[i];
    float r = red(c), g = green(c), b = blue(c);
    if (r < minR) minR = r;
    if (g < minG) minG = g;
    if (b < minB) minB = b;
    if (r > maxR) maxR = r;
    if (g > maxG) maxG = g;
    if (b > maxB) maxB = b;
  }

  for (int i = 0; i < img.pixels.length; i++) {
    color c = img.pixels[i];
    float r = red(c), g = green(c), b = blue(c);

    if (perChannel) {
      r = map(r, minR, maxR, 0, 255);
      g = map(g, minG, maxG, 0, 255);
      b = map(b, minB, maxB, 0, 255);
    } else {
      float minAll = min(minR, min(minG, minB));
      float maxAll = max(maxR, max(maxG, maxB));
      r = map(r, minAll, maxAll, 0, 255);
      g = map(g, minAll, maxAll, 0, 255);
      b = map(b, minAll, maxAll, 0, 255);
    }

    out.pixels[i] = color(constrain(r, 0, 255), constrain(g, 0, 255), constrain(b, 0, 255));
  }

  out.updatePixels();
  return out;
}


•	Call with perChannel = true → Auto Levels (independent per channel).
•	Call with perChannel = false → Auto Contrast (preserves balance).

⸻

3. Optional Photoshop-style refinements • Photoshop usually clips 0.5–1% of the darkest/brightest pixels instead of using exact min/max (to avoid noise). You can implement this by sorting pixel values or building a histogram and ignoring the extreme ends. • Gamma adjustment in Photoshop’s Levels isn’t part of “Auto Levels” but you can add it by applying an exponent to midtones.

⸻

✅ **Summary:** • For blending many layers, use *averaging or low opacity*. • For Photoshop-style cleanup, you can implement *Auto Levels* or *Auto Contrast* by rescaling pixel ranges.

⸻

Would you like me to add an autoLevels() pass directly into the permutation composite pipeline so that every saved output is normalized automatically?

Added! The pipeline now includes an Auto Levels/Auto Contrast pass (with percentile clipping) and a simple “average all layers” option for low-opacity looks.

Downloads: • Processing sketch with Auto Levels (.pde) • HTML overview of the updated approach

What changed (quick): • BLEND_STRATEGY: "SEQUENTIAL" (stack with modes) or "AVERAGE" (equal-weight mean so all images contribute). • AUTO_LEVELS: true/false to normalize results. • AUTO_LEVELS_PER_CHANNEL: false ≈ Auto Contrast (preserve color balance); true ≈ Auto Levels (per-channel scaling). • AUTO_CLIP_PERCENT: clip 0.5% shadows/highlights by default to avoid noise-driven extremes.

Want me to switch the generator to combinations (one composite per set, not per order) or add a CSV to control per-layer mode/opacity?

Done. I added CSV-driven per-layer rules (mode, opacity, weight) and an example file.

Downloads: • Processing sketch with CSV support (.pde) • HTML: CSV overview & instructions • Example layer_rules.csv

How it works (quick): • Put your CSV at data/layer_rules.csv with headers: layer,mode,opacity,weight • layer is 1-based (1 = bottom/base, n = top). • mode is used in SEQUENTIAL strategy (leave blank to fall back to your MODES cycle/default). • opacity (0–1) applies per layer in SEQUENTIAL (blank → LAYER_OPACITY). • weight (≥0) applies in AVERAGE strategy; all weights are normalized (blank → 1.0). • Switch strategy via BLEND_STRATEGY = "SEQUENTIAL" or "AVERAGE".

If you want, I can also: • add a manifest CSV that maps image index → filename for traceability, and • a combinations generator (one composite per set, not per order).

Add manifest csv

Perfect — a manifest CSV makes the outputs traceable.

I’ll extend the sketch so that for every permutation processed, it also appends a row to output/manifest.csv with:

filename order strategy modes auto_levels source_files

•	**filename**: The PNG output file.
•	**order**: Index order (e.g. 0-2-1).
•	**strategy**: SEQUENTIAL or AVERAGE.
•	**modes**: The blend modes used (from CSV or default).
•	**auto_levels**: AutoLevels, AutoContrast, or None.
•	**source_files**: Comma-separated original file names for the chosen order.

⸻

✨ **How it will work** • On the first run, the sketch will create output/manifest.csv with a header. • Each permutation processed appends a line. • You can open manifest.csv in Excel/Numbers to see exactly how each composite was built.

⸻

Would you like me to integrate the manifest into the current CSV-enabled sketch (so it writes both images + manifest at once), or make a standalone manifest generator that scans the already-created outputs and rebuilds the table afterward?

Integrate

Integrated. Each saved composite now logs a row to output/manifest.csv.

Downloads: • Processing sketch with Manifest integration (.pde) • HTML: Manifest overview

What you get in manifest.csv: • filename — output PNG name • order — index order like 0-2-1 (matching sorted data/input load order) • strategy — SEQUENTIAL or AVERAGE • modes — per-layer modes (e.g., BASE-MULTIPLY-SOFTLIGHT-…) or N/A for AVERAGE • auto_levels — AutoLevels, AutoContrast, or None • source_files — filenames in the applied order

If you want extra columns (e.g., per-layer opacity or weights), I can add those too.

Add a gui to the processing sketch that allows user to select image source folder and destination folder for the composites

Done — added a simple in-sketch GUI so you can choose the source and output folders before generating.

Downloads: • Processing sketch with folder-select GUI (.pde) • HTML overview for the GUI build

How it works: • Run the sketch → click Select SOURCE folder… (pick the folder with your images). • Click Select OUTPUT folder… (where composites + manifest.csv will be saved). • Click START GENERATION to run the permutations pipeline.

Extras kept from earlier versions: • Optional layer_rules.csv is auto-detected in the chosen SOURCE folder. • Manifest logging to OUTPUT/manifest.csv. • Auto-Levels/Auto-Contrast with percentile clipping. • Two blend strategies (SEQUENTIAL vs AVERAGE) — still set via constants at the top for now.

If you want on-canvas toggles (dropdowns/sliders) for strategy, opacity, clip %, Auto Levels on/off, I can add them next.

Add an option to the processing sketch to simplify the workflow. This option will use source images all UHD size, greyscale. So the images are all 3840 × 2160 pixels.

Added a UHD greyscale option to simplify your workflow.

Downloads: • Processing sketch with UHD greyscale option (.pde) • HTML: overview of the UHD option

What’s new (quick): • UHD_MODE = true enables a streamlined path that assumes all inputs are 3840×2160. • UHD_STRICT • true: throws an error if any input isn’t exactly 3840×2160. • false: auto-resizes any non-UHD image to UHD. • UHD_FORCE_GREYSCALE = true force-converts each image to greyscale on load. • Keeps everything else: GUI folder pickers, CSV per-layer rules, manifest logging, Auto Levels/Contrast, and both blend strategies.

Want me to add on-canvas toggles for switching UHD mode, strictness, and greyscale on/off without editing code?

Can processing cope with UHD source images?

Yes — Processing can handle UHD (3840 × 2160) source images, but there are a few caveats depending on your machine, memory settings, and the exact operations you do.

⸻

⚙️ **1. Image Size in Memory** • A single **3840×2160** image has **8.3 million pixels**. • At 4 bytes per pixel (ARGB), that’s **~33 MB per image in RAM**. • With 10 source images, you’re already at ~330 MB just for the pixel arrays (not counting overhead, composites, and temp buffers). 👉 With multiple permutations and copies, memory usage can grow into the **GBs quickly**.

⸻

🧵 **2. Processing’s Pixel Operations** • PImage.loadPixels() → creates an int[] of length width*height. • Operations like blending or auto-levels iterate over the full array each time. For UHD, that means looping through ~8.3 million values per layer per pass. • On a modern CPU, it’s feasible, but each composite may take **seconds rather than milliseconds**. 👉 If you’re generating *all* permutations of even 6 UHD images (720 composites), it could take **hours**.

⸻

💻 **3. Practical Strategies** • **Memory allocation:** Increase Processing’s Java heap size (Preferences → Run → VM Options or processing-java --jvm-args). e.g. -Xmx8G for 8 GB. • **Batching:** Add MAX_PERMS to limit how many permutations you render in one run. • **Average strategy:** Much lighter than sequential blending (since it just sums pixel channels once). • **Save intermediates:** Write images to disk as soon as possible instead of keeping them all in RAM. • **Downsample preview mode:** Work at 25–50% scale while tuning, then switch back to UHD. • **Consider GPU/FFmpeg:** For very large batch composites, shaders or external tools (e.g. FFmpeg + blend filters) can be orders of magnitude faster.

⸻

✅ **Summary** • **Yes, Processing can cope with UHD images.** • You’ll need: plenty of RAM, possibly increased Java heap, and realistic limits on permutations. • For “few dozen composites” it’s fine. For “hundreds of UHD permutations,” expect long runtimes and high memory load.

⸻

Would you like me to prepare a dual-resolution mode in the sketch (e.g. preview at 960×540, final export at 3840×2160) so you can experiment quickly but still output UHD when ready?

Yes, and provide an HTML explanation

All set — I added a dual-resolution workflow with a PREVIEW (scaled) button and a FINAL export, plus an HTML explainer.

Downloads: • Processing sketch — dual-res preview & export (.pde) • HTML — Dual-Resolution overview

How to use it: 1. Open the sketch → choose SOURCE and OUTPUT folders. 2. Set options (UHD mode/strict/greyscale, blend strategy, opacity, Auto Levels, clip %, and Preview Scale). 3. Click PREVIEW (scaled) to generate quick low-res outputs (filenames include __scale_0.25, etc.). 4. Click EXPORT FINAL to render full-res (UHD or full source size, with __scale_1.00).

The manifest still logs everything to OUTPUT/manifest.csv so previews and finals are traceable. Want me to add a progress bar or a “first N permutations only” control next?

Yes add progress bar and first N

What is the meaning of stride?

Great question. In this context:

Stride • A stride means you step through a sequence with a fixed interval instead of taking every element. • Mathematically, if you have a list of items indexed 0, 1, 2, 3, …, and your stride is n, you only keep those where index % n == 0.

⸻

Examples • Suppose you have 10 permutations: P0, P1, P2, …, P9. • Stride = 1 → keep all (P0…P9). • Stride = 2 → keep every 2nd: (P0, P2, P4, P6, P8). • Stride = 3 → keep every 3rd: (P0, P3, P6, P9).

⸻

Why use stride here? • If you have a huge set (e.g. 720 permutations), rendering all can be slow. • With stride = 10, you generate ~10% of them (72 images), evenly spaced across the whole set. • It’s like skimming through the permutation space in a regular, systematic way, rather than taking everything.

⸻

👉 In your Processing sketch, the “1-in-n” option is just another way of saying **stride = n**.

What is seeded random subset?

Good question 👍

A seeded random subset means: • Instead of taking permutations evenly spaced by a stride (every 10th, every 20th), you pick a random sample of them. • Seeded means you use a fixed random seed (a starting value for the random number generator). That way: • With the same seed, you’ll always get the same subset. • With a different seed, you’ll get a different (but still reproducible) subset.

⸻

📊 **Example**

You have 720 permutations of 6 images. • Stride = 10 → you get permutations #0, #10, #20, … evenly spaced (deterministic, but patterned). • Random subset, seed = 1234, 10% → you get 72 permutations chosen randomly across the whole set. • Run again with the same seed (1234) → identical 72. • Change the seed to 5678 → a different 72, still reproducible.

⸻

✅ **Why useful** • If you want **variety**, stride may look too regular. • A seeded random subset lets you **sample the space more naturally**, but still keep reproducibility (so you can regenerate or extend results later).

⸻
